section.title: The Crucifixion and Burial
section.sectionRationale: The hour toward which the whole Gospel has moved arrives. Jesus is flogged, mocked as a king, and handed over to be crucified. On the cross He entrusts His mother to the beloved disciple and, having accomplished all, cries, It is finished, and yields up His spirit. John draws out the fulfillment of Scripture at every turn: the unbroken bones of the Passover lamb, the pierced side, the parted garments, and a burial by Joseph and Nicodemus in a garden tomb.
section.thesis: The King of the Jews reigns from a cross, bearing mockery and death in sovereign obedience, and having accomplished all the Father gave Him He declares it finished and lays down His life, His unbroken bones and pierced side marking Him as the true Passover Lamb whose death, foretold in Scripture, is complete and is sealed by an honorable burial.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the flogging, mockery, and Pilate's Here is the man; the sentence and the crucifixion with its inscription; the parted garments and the care for His mother; the accomplished work, It is finished, and the pierced side; and the burial in the garden tomb.

verses.text: Then Pilate took Jesus and had Him flogged.
The soldiers twisted together a crown of thorns, set it on His head, and dressed Him in a purple robe.
And they went up to Him again and again, saying, “Hail, King of the Jews!” and slapping Him in the face.
Once again Pilate came out and said to the Jews, “Look, I am bringing Him out to you to let you know that I find no basis for a charge against Him.”
When Jesus came out wearing the crown of thorns and the purple robe, Pilate said to them, “Here is the man!”
As soon as the chief priests and officers saw Him, they shouted, “Crucify Him! Crucify Him!” “You take Him and crucify Him,” Pilate replied, “for I find no basis for a charge against Him.”
“We have a law,” answered the Jews, “and according to that law He must die, because He declared Himself to be the Son of God.”
When Pilate heard this statement, he was even more afraid,
and he went back into the Praetorium. “Where are You from?” he asked. But Jesus gave no answer.
So Pilate said to Him, “Do You refuse to speak to me? Do You not know that I have authority to release You and authority to crucify You?”
Jesus answered, “You would have no authority over Me if it were not given to you from above. Therefore the one who handed Me over to you is guilty of greater sin.”
From then on, Pilate tried to release Him, but the Jews kept shouting, “If you release this man, you are no friend of Caesar. Anyone who declares himself a king is defying Caesar.”
When Pilate heard these words, he brought Jesus out and sat on the judgment seat at a place called the Stone Pavement, which in Hebrew is Gabbatha.
It was the day of Preparation for the Passover, about the sixth hour. And Pilate said to the Jews, “Here is your {{basileus|King}}!”
At this, they shouted, “Away with Him! Away with Him! Crucify Him!” “Shall I crucify your King?” Pilate asked. “We have no king but Caesar,” replied the chief priests.
Then Pilate handed Jesus over to be crucified, and the soldiers took Him away.
Carrying His own cross, He went out to The Place of the Skull, which in Hebrew is called Golgotha.
There they crucified Him, and with Him two others, one on each side, with Jesus in the middle.
Pilate also had a notice posted on the cross. It read: JESUS OF NAZARETH, THE KING OF THE JEWS.
Many of the Jews read this sign, because the place where Jesus was crucified was near the city, and it was written in Hebrew, Latin, and Greek.
So the chief priests of the Jews said to Pilate, “Do not write, ‘The King of the Jews,’ but only that He said, ‘I am the King of the Jews.’”
Pilate answered, “What I have written, I have written.”
When the soldiers had crucified Jesus, they divided His garments into four parts, one for each soldier, with the tunic remaining. It was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom.

So they said to one another, “Let us not tear it. Instead, let us cast lots to see who will get it.” This was to fulfill the Scripture: “They divided My garments among them, and cast lots for My clothing.” So that is what the soldiers did.
Near the cross of Jesus stood His mother and her sister, as well as Mary the wife of Clopas and Mary Magdalene.
When Jesus saw His mother and the disciple whom He loved standing nearby, He said to His mother, “Woman, here is your son.”
Then He said to the disciple, “Here is your mother.” So from that hour, this disciple took her into his home.

units.label: It is finished
units.note: the Lamb whose bones are not broken

verses.text: After this, knowing that everything had now been accomplished, and to fulfill the Scripture, Jesus said, “I am thirsty.”
A jar of sour wine was sitting there. So they soaked a sponge in the wine, put it on a stalk of {{hyssopos|hyssop}}, and lifted it to His mouth.
When Jesus had received the sour wine, He said, “{{teleo|It is finished}}.” And bowing His head, He yielded up His spirit.

It was the day of Preparation, and the next day was a High Sabbath. In order that the bodies would not remain on the cross during the Sabbath, the Jews asked Pilate to have the legs broken and the bodies removed.
So the soldiers came and broke the legs of the first man who had been crucified with Jesus, and those of the other.
But when they came to Jesus and saw that He was already dead, they did not break His legs.
Instead, one of the soldiers pierced His side with a spear, and immediately blood and water flowed out.
The one who saw it has testified to this, and his testimony is true. He knows that he is telling the truth, so that you also may believe.
Now these things happened so that the Scripture would be fulfilled: “Not one of His bones will be broken.”
And, as another Scripture says: “They will look on the One they have pierced.”

units.label: The burial
units.note: a new tomb in a garden

verses.text: Afterward, Joseph of Arimathea, who was a disciple of Jesus (but secretly for fear of the Jews), asked Pilate to let him remove the body of Jesus. Pilate gave him permission, so he came and removed His body.
Nicodemus, who had previously come to Jesus at night, also brought a mixture of myrrh and aloes, about seventy-five pounds.
So they took the body of Jesus and wrapped it in linen cloths with the spices, according to the Jewish burial custom.
Now there was a garden in the place where Jesus was crucified, and in the garden a new tomb in which no one had yet been laid.
And because it was the Jewish day of Preparation and the tomb was nearby, they placed Jesus there.

basileus.gloss: King. The title saturates the crucifixion: the soldiers mock Him as King of the Jews, Pilate presents Him as your King, and the charge posted on the cross in three languages proclaims Him King. John's irony is profound: the mockery states the truth. Jesus really is the King, reigning from the cross, and the world unknowingly proclaims His royalty even while crucifying Him.

hyssopos.gloss: Hyssop, the small plant used to daub the Passover lamb's blood on the doorframes in Exodus 12. John notes the sour wine was lifted on hyssop, a deliberate Passover echo. As Jesus dies at the time the Passover lambs are prepared, the detail marks Him as the true Passover Lamb whose blood shields His people from death.

teleo.gloss: To finish, complete, accomplish, bring to its intended goal. Jesus' cry, It is finished, is a single word in Greek, tetelestai, a shout of completion, not defeat. The word was used for paying a debt in full. The saving work the Father gave Him is done, the Scriptures fulfilled, the price paid; nothing remains to be added.

basic.context: Pilate has Jesus flogged, and the soldiers crown Him with thorns and mock Him as king. Pilate keeps declaring Him innocent, yet, pressured by the threat that releasing a self-styled king makes him no friend of Caesar, he hands Jesus over. Jesus carries His cross to Golgotha and is crucified between two others, with a sign proclaiming Him King of the Jews in three languages. From the cross He entrusts His mother to the beloved disciple. Knowing all was accomplished, He cries, It is finished, and yields up His spirit. His bones are not broken and His side is pierced, fulfilling Scripture, and Joseph and Nicodemus bury Him in a new garden tomb.
basic.hermeneutics: John presents the cross as the throne and the hour of glory, not merely tragedy. The recurring title King and the trilingual sign proclaim Jesus' royalty even in mockery. The key words are It is finished, a cry of completion. Watch how John points repeatedly to fulfilled Scripture, the unbroken bones and pierced side marking Jesus as the Passover Lamb. The blood and water and the eyewitness testimony are given so that we may believe.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter is saturated with kingship: Jesus is mocked and proclaimed basileus, King, and the truth shines through the irony. The hyssopos, hyssop, that lifts the wine recalls the Passover. And His final cry is a single Greek word, tetelestai, from teleo, it is finished, the shout that the saving work is complete.
basic.typology: Jesus is the true Passover Lamb: He dies as the lambs are prepared, His bones are not broken as the Passover lamb's must not be, and hyssop appears as at the first Passover. The pierced side fulfills Zechariah's prophecy. The garden tomb echoes the garden where the story of humanity began.
basic.christ: Christ reigns from the cross as King, the innocent Son of God condemned in the place of sinners. He is the Passover Lamb whose death shields His people, and He completes the saving work with the cry, It is finished, laying down His life in sovereign obedience.
basic.god: God is sovereign over the whole scene; even Pilate's authority is given from above. The Father's saving plan, foretold in Scripture, is accomplished exactly, so that the cross is no accident but the fulfillment of God's purpose to save.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 12 gives the Passover lamb and hyssop. Psalm 22 foretells the parted garments and thirst. Zechariah 12 foretells the pierced One. Isaiah 53 describes the servant's death and burial with the rich.
basic.oneStory: The King of the world reigns from a cross, the true Passover Lamb whose blood delivers His people. When He cries, It is finished, the saving work promised since the fall is complete; the Scriptures are fulfilled to the last detail, and He is laid in a garden tomb, ready for the dawn of new creation.

context.literary: The center of John's Passion, structured by movements before Pilate and then at the cross, heavy with the theme of kingship and the repeated fulfillment of Scripture. John foregrounds Jesus' sovereign self-possession, His care for His mother, and the eyewitness testimony to the pierced side. The cry It is finished forms the climax, and the honorable burial by two secret disciples closes the movement, preparing for the resurrection.
context.historical: Roman flogging was brutal, and crucifixion was a public, shameful death reserved for slaves and rebels. The trilingual inscription reflects the Roman practice of posting the charge. Breaking the legs hastened death; John notes it was omitted for Jesus because He was already dead. The day of Preparation and the timing about noon connect Jesus' death to the preparation of the Passover lambs. The lavish spices Nicodemus brought befit a royal burial.
context.canonical: The crucifixion fulfills a cluster of Scriptures John cites: Psalm 22:18 for the garments, Psalm 69:21 for the thirst, Psalm 34:20 with Exodus 12:46 for the unbroken bones, and Zechariah 12:10 for the pierced One. The Passover Lamb typology runs from Exodus 12 to 1 Corinthians 5:7, Christ our Passover. It is finished declares the completed atonement expounded in Hebrews 9 and 10, and the blood and water have been seen as signs of the cleansing and life that flow from Christ's death, 1 John 5:6-8.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the crucified Jesus as the true King reigning from the cross and the true Passover Lamb whose death completes the saving work of God, fulfilling Scripture in exact detail, and to call readers, through the eyewitness testimony, to believe.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the historical crucifixion and burial. Its abiding truths are that Jesus reigns as King through the cross, that His death is the finished, once-for-all atonement, and that He is the Passover Lamb; the eyewitness testimony is given precisely so that readers may believe and live.

debates.question: What is the meaning of It is finished?

views.label: The atoning work is complete
views.case: The single word tetelestai declares that the saving work the Father gave Jesus is accomplished, the debt paid in full, so that nothing can be added to His finished sacrifice.

views.label: The Scriptures are fulfilled
views.case: John repeatedly notes fulfillment, so the cry also announces that all that was written of the Messiah's suffering has now come to pass; the plan of redemption is brought to its goal.

debates.bearing: Both belong together: the cry declares that the atonement is complete and that every Scripture about the suffering Christ is fulfilled, so the cross is a finished, sufficient work, not a defeat.

debates.question: What is signified by the blood and water from Jesus' side?

views.label: Proof of a real, physical death
views.case: The flow of blood and water is medical evidence that Jesus truly died, which the eyewitness stresses so that readers may believe the reality of His death and, therefore, His resurrection.

views.label: A sign of cleansing and life
views.case: Many see the blood and water as symbols of the atonement and the Spirit, the cleansing and life that flow from Christ's death, echoed in 1 John 5:6-8.

debates.bearing: Both may be held: the blood and water confirm a genuine death and, richly, signify the cleansing and life that pour from the crucified Savior to His people.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter is soaked in kingship: Jesus is mocked as basileus, King, crowned with thorns, robed in purple, and hailed with false homage, while Pilate presents Him as your King and posts the charge, the King of the Jews, in three languages, so that the mockery unwittingly proclaims the truth. As He dies at the hour the Passover lambs are readied, the sour wine is lifted to Him on hyssopos, hyssop, the very plant used to apply the Passover blood, marking Him as the true Lamb. And His last word is a single Greek term, tetelestai, from teleo, it is finished, a shout of completion, not collapse: the debt is paid, the work is done, the Scriptures are fulfilled.

typology.type: The Passover lamb, its bones unbroken, its blood applied by hyssop
typology.fulfillment: Christ, dying as the lambs are prepared, His bones unbroken, marked by hyssop, the true Passover Lamb, Exodus 12 and John 19:29, 33, 36.
The soldier piercing Jesus' side, at whom the world will look, John 19:34 and 37.

typology.type: The garden of Eden and the garden tomb
typology.fulfillment: Jesus laid in a garden tomb, where new creation will dawn, undoing the exile from the first garden, John 19:41.

study.christ: Christ reigns from the cross as the true King, proclaimed in three languages even by His enemies. Flogged, crowned with thorns, and mocked, He remains sovereign, reminding Pilate that all authority is given from above and tending His mother in His dying moments. He is the innocent Son of God, condemned in the place of sinners, and the true Passover Lamb whose bones are not broken and whose blood shields His people. When His saving work is complete, He does not merely expire but declares, It is finished, and deliberately yields up His spirit, so that even His death is an act of sovereign obedience and completed love.
study.god: God is sovereign over every detail of the crucifixion. Pilate has no authority except what is given from above, and the whole scene unfolds by the Father's determined plan, foretold in Scripture and now fulfilled to the letter. The unbroken bones, the pierced side, the parted garments, and the thirst all answer ancient words, showing that the cross is no tragedy that overtook God but the accomplishment of His long purpose to save. In the death of His Son, God provides the true Passover, delivering His people from judgment, and He begins, in a garden tomb, to make all things new.

crossReferences.note: The Passover lamb's bones must not be broken, fulfilled in Jesus, the true Passover Lamb.
They divide My garments and cast lots, fulfilled by the soldiers at the cross.
They will look on Me, the One they have pierced, fulfilled when the soldier pierced Jesus' side.
Christ our Passover lamb has been sacrificed, the reality John's details proclaim.

study.oneStory: The King of the world reigns from a cross, proclaimed King even by the mocking sign above His head, and He dies as the true Passover Lamb at the very hour the lambs are prepared, His blood delivering His people from death as the first Passover foreshadowed. When He cries, It is finished, the saving work promised since the fall is complete, and the Scriptures are fulfilled to the last detail, the unbroken bones, the pierced side, the parted garments. Laid in a new tomb in a garden, the King awaits the dawn, where the story that began in a garden will be answered by the new creation about to break.

quiz.q: How does John present Jesus' kingship at the crucifixion?

options.text: Even the mockery and the trilingual sign unwittingly proclaim the truth that Jesus is King.
options.note: The recurring title and the inscription state the truth through irony; He reigns from the cross.

options.text: Jesus finally abandons any claim to be a king.
options.note: The chapter presents Him as King throughout, even in the inscription Pilate refuses to change.

options.text: The kingship is merely a joke with no deeper meaning.
options.note: The soldiers mean it as mockery, but John shows it states the truth of His reign.

options.text: He is crowned and robed as a king in mockery.
options.note: True, but it misses John's irony that the mock kingship proclaims His real royalty.

quiz.q: What does Jesus' cry It is finished mean?

options.text: The saving work the Father gave Him is complete, a shout of accomplishment, not defeat.
options.note: The single word tetelestai declares the atonement finished and the Scriptures fulfilled.

options.text: He had endured all He could bear.
options.note: He did not merely run out of strength; He deliberately yielded up His spirit once the work was accomplished.

options.text: He was giving up on His mission.
options.note: The opposite; the mission is accomplished, not abandoned.

options.text: His suffering was at last over.
options.note: His suffering did end, but the cry means the saving work itself is completed, not merely that pain stopped.

quiz.q: Why does John stress that Jesus' bones were not broken?

options.text: To show He is the true Passover Lamb, whose bones must not be broken.
options.note: Verse 36 cites the Passover Scripture; Jesus fulfills the lamb typology.

options.text: Because it was a lucky coincidence.
options.note: John presents it as the deliberate fulfillment of Scripture, not chance.

options.text: Because He was already dead, so breaking them was not needed.
options.note: That is why they did not break them, but John's point is the fulfillment of the Passover Scripture that no bone would be broken.

options.text: To show Scripture was being fulfilled.
options.note: True, but it omits the specific Passover Lamb typology the detail proclaims.

quiz.q: What did Jesus do for His mother from the cross?

options.text: He entrusted her to the care of the beloved disciple.
options.note: Verses 26 and 27 show His tender provision for His mother even while dying.

options.text: He arranged for others to look after her later.
options.note: He did provide for her care, but by entrusting her then and there to the beloved disciple as his own mother.

options.text: He ignored her in His suffering.
options.note: He deliberately cared for her amid His agony.

options.text: He commended her to a disciple's household.
options.note: True, but it slightly understates the tenderness of Woman, here is your son, and here is your mother.

quiz.q: How is Jesus buried, according to the chapter?

options.text: Joseph and Nicodemus wrap Him with abundant spices and lay Him in a new garden tomb.
options.note: Verses 38 to 42 describe the honorable burial in a new tomb.

options.text: He is left unburied on the cross.
options.note: He is taken down and buried with care by Joseph and Nicodemus.

options.text: He is buried hastily with no special care.
options.note: He was buried in haste before the Sabbath, but with lavish spices in a new garden tomb, an honorable burial.

options.text: Two secret disciples give Him a proper burial.
options.note: True, but it omits the detail of the new garden tomb and the abundant spices, marking an honorable, even royal, burial.

questions: The King of the world reigned from a cross, crowned with thorns. How does this upside-down kingship reshape your idea of power and greatness?
Jesus reminded Pilate that his authority was given from above. How does it steady you to know God is sovereign even over those who seem to hold power over you?
It is finished was a cry of completion. What are you tempted to add to Christ's finished work to feel accepted, and how does His cry free you?
Jesus is the true Passover Lamb whose blood shields His people. How does knowing your deliverance was bought at such cost move your gratitude?
Even in agony Jesus cared for His mother. How does His attentiveness to one person in the midst of His suffering encourage you to trust His care for you?

translationNotes.note: The day of Preparation for the Passover, about the sixth hour; the BSB footnote gives about noon. Jesus is condemned as the Passover lambs are being prepared, underscoring the Lamb typology.
The sour wine was lifted on a stalk of hyssop, the plant used to apply the Passover blood in Exodus 12:22, a deliberate echo, and fulfilling Psalm 69:21.
It is finished renders the single Greek word tetelestai, a shout of completion used for a debt paid in full; the saving work is accomplished.
Not one of His bones will be broken cites Psalm 34:20 and the Passover law of Exodus 12:46; They will look on the One they have pierced cites Zechariah 12:10.
